# **Architectural Decomposition and Stochastic Recourse in Hierarchical Production Planning: A Selective Literature Review**

The development of mathematically rigorous decision support systems for manufacturing operations requires aligning tactical, aggregate plans with short-term, daily execution schedules.1 In complex process industries, such as food and beverage manufacturing, this alignment is complicated by multi-level Bill of Materials (BOM) dependencies, capacity constraints, sequence-dependent setup times, and demand volatility.1 This report presents an exhaustive literature analysis designed to support a three-layer hierarchical stochastic production planning system deployed at a Turkish business-to-business (B2B) bubble tea manufacturer.  
The manufacturing system under consideration features four distinct products, two parallel production lines, and a multi-level BOM where an intermediate product—Semi-Finished Boba—is both sold independently and consumed internally as an input by two finished products.1 The three mathematical layers of the decision support system are structured as follows:

* **Layer 1:** A monthly, twelve-month horizon, two-stage stochastic aggregate model where Months 1–2 represent deterministic first-stage decisions, and Months 3–12 model scenario-dependent recourse under demand uncertainty.5  
* **Layer 2:** An eight-week rolling horizon, daily Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) scheduling model where production quantity is fully determined by binary machine-assignment decisions, subject to pasteurization exclusivity, two-tier backlogging penalties, and linking inventory targets from Layer 1 at Days 24 and 48\.1  
* **Layer 3:** A weekly rolling horizon execution framework that stabilizes near-term decisions and prevents excessive schedule nervousness.2

In empirical testing, a full-year daily exact formulation proved computationally intractable, exceeding 2,200 seconds of solver time, thereby motivating the hierarchical decomposition strategy.9 The deployed system achieved a ![][image1] total cost reduction compared to a deterministic planning approach, reduced average overtime days from ![][image2] to ![][image3] days across ten scenarios, and demonstrated out-of-sample stochastic dominance in ![][image4] of test cases (with an average advantage of ![][image5] over ![][image6] iterations).7 This review evaluates the foundational literature supporting this mathematical and operational architecture.

## **Q1: Canonical Rolling Horizon References in Operations Research**

The operational execution of both Layer 1 and Layer 2 relies on rolling horizon procedures, where a multi-period model is solved iteratively, immediate decisions are executed, and the planning window is shifted forward in time.9 Academic literature has established several foundational papers that define and mathematically validate this receding horizon strategy.

### **1\. Seminal Experimental Foundation**

* **Best Citation:** Kenneth R. Baker, "An experimental study of the effectiveness of rolling schedules in production planning," *Decision Sciences*, vol. 8, no. 1, pp. 19-27, 1977\.11 DOI: 10.1111/j.1540-5915.1977.tb01067.x.  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~374.12  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** High-quality decision sciences journal; highly respected in operations management.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This is the first explicit academic work to experimentally evaluate the cost efficiency of rolling schedules in multi-period lot-sizing.13 It proves that executing only first-period decisions on a rolling basis provides near-optimal cost performance (within ![][image7] of the global deterministic optimum), justifying the rolling execution of Layer 3\.13  
* **Confidence:** High.12

### **2\. Analytical Control-Theoretic Foundation**

* **Best Citation:** Suresh Sethi and Gerhard Sorger, "A theory of rolling horizon decision making," *Annals of Operations Research*, vol. 29, no. 1, pp. 387-415, 1991\.14 DOI: 10.1007/BF02283610.  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~354.15  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Reputable, core operations research journal.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This paper provides the formal dynamic control theory for rolling horizon decision-making.9 It mathematically defines and proves the existence of "forecast horizons" and "decision horizons," which guarantees that terminal boundary distortions do not propagate backward to affect immediate operational actions.9  
* **Confidence:** High.14

### **3\. Bibliographic and Taxonomic Survey**

* **Best Citation:** Suresh Chand, Vernon Ning Hsu, and Suresh P. Sethi, "Forecast, solution, and rolling horizons in operations management problems: A classified bibliography," *Manufacturing & Service Operations Management* (M\&SOM), vol. 4, no. 1, pp. 25-43, 2002\.17 DOI: 10.1287/msom.4.1.25.283.18  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~314.17  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Elite, top-tier operations management journal (UTD24/FT50 list).  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This paper serves as the primary literature-mapping reference for rolling horizon procedures in operations management, classifying over ![][image8] papers across multiple inventory and production planning dimensions.9  
* **Confidence:** High.17

### **4\. Corrective Analysis of Sahin et al. (2013)**

* **Best Citation:** Funda Sahin, Arunachalam Narayanan, and E. Powell Robinson, "Rolling horizon planning in supply chains: Review, implications and directions for future research," *International Journal of Production Research* (IJPR), vol. 51, no. 18, pp. 5413-5436, 2013\.20 DOI: 10.1080/00207543.2013.775523.20  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~115.21  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Top-tier international journal in manufacturing, logistics, and industrial engineering.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** The candidate paper "Sahin, Süral & Denizel (2013) in IJPR" does *not* exist; instead, the correct paper is Sahin, Narayanan, and Robinson (2013).20 The user's candidate likely conflates "Funda Sahin" with "Haldun Süral" and "Meltem Denizel," who co-authored other lot-sizing papers.22 This actual 2013 paper reviews how rolling schedules behave under demand uncertainty, focusing on the trade-offs between schedule nervousness, inventory costs, and service levels.8  
* **Confidence:** High.20

| Reference | Scope of Study | Major Theoretical Contribution |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Baker (1977)** 12 | Lot-sizing under dynamic demand | Proved that rolling schedules are highly cost-efficient and robust to horizon length.13 |
| **Sethi & Sorger (1991)** 14 | Dynamic optimal control theory | Formulated analytical criteria for the stability and convergence of rolling horizons.9 |
| **Chand et al. (2002)** 17 | Bibliographic taxonomy | Categorized the dynamic horizon properties of inventory and scheduling models.19 |
| **Sahin et al. (2013)** 20 | Coordinated supply chains | Analyzed lot-sizing instability and the impact of information-sharing on rolling plans.8 |

## **Q2: Advanced Mathematical Formulations for Stochastic Multi-Level Capacitated Lot-Sizing**

The tactical Layer 1 model represents a stochastic variant of the Multi-Level Capacitated Lot-Sizing Problem (MLCLSP), where monthly production setups and inventory targets must buffer against future demand volatility across a multi-level BOM.26 The top papers formulating these stochastic variants are detailed below.

### **1\. The Gold-Standard for Stochastic Multi-Level Lot-Sizing**

* **Best Citation:** Simon Thevenin, Yossiri Adulyasak, and Jean-François Cordeau, "Material Requirements Planning Under Demand Uncertainty Using Stochastic Optimization," *Production and Operations Management*, vol. 30, no. 2, pp. 475-493, 2021\.6 DOI: 10.1111/poms.13289.  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~91.28  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Elite, top-tier operations and supply chain management journal (UTD24/FT50 list).  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This study explicitly formulates two-stage and multi-stage stochastic capacitated lot-sizing models under demand uncertainty.6 It establishes the "static-dynamic" decision framework, where binary setup decisions are frozen in the first stage, while production and consumption quantities serve as second-stage recourse decisions.6 This matches Layer 1's architecture, where first-stage months 1–2 lock in setup commitments, leaving months 3–12 for scenario-dependent volume recourse.5  
* **Confidence:** High.6

### **2\. Multi-Stage Stochastic Master Production Scheduling**

* **Best Citation:** Erkut Körpeoğlu, Hande Yaman, and M. Selim Aktürk, "A multi-stage stochastic programming approach in master production scheduling," *European Journal of Operational Research* (EJOR), vol. 213, no. 1, pp. 166-179, 2011\.30 DOI: 10.1016/j.ejor.2011.03.004.31  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~58.30  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Top-tier operations research journal.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This paper formulates a stochastic aggregate scheduling model under demand and capacity uncertainty.30 It models setup choices as here-and-now decisions, utilizing controllable processing times as an operational recourse option to compress manufacturing times when demand spikes, which aligns with our Layer 1 and Layer 2 recourse formulations.30  
* **Confidence:** High.30

### **3\. Multi-Level Stochastic Decomposition and Replenishment**

* **Best Citation:** Matthieu Gruson, Jean-François Cordeau, and Raf Jans, "Benders decomposition for a stochastic three-level lot sizing and replenishment problem with a distribution structure," *European Journal of Operational Research* (EJOR), vol. 291, no. 1, pp. 206-217, 2021\.33 DOI: 10.1016/j.ejor.2020.09.025.  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~26-32.34  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Top-tier operations research journal.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This study addresses multi-stage demand uncertainty in a multi-level lot-sizing and replenishment system.33 By applying Benders decomposition, it decouples the binary setup variables from continuous volume and inventory recourse variables, providing a mathematical blueprint for managing multi-level BOM dependencies under capacity limits.33  
* **Confidence:** High.33

| Reference | Uncertainty Modeling | Recourse Strategy | Solver Methodology |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Thevenin et al. (2021)** 6 | Scenario-based demand uncertainty | Setup freezing (static setup, dynamic volumes) 6 | Sample Average Approximation (SAA) & Heuristics 6 |
| **Körpeoğlu et al. (2011)** 30 | Demand and capacity scenarios 31 | Controllable processing times (capacity compression) 30 | Deterministic equivalent compact model 31 |
| **Gruson et al. (2021)** 33 | Scenario trees for multi-level demand | Replenishment volume adjustment 35 | Benders Decomposition & Matheuristics 33 |

## **Q3: Food and Beverage Lot-Sizing and Scheduling Case Studies**

Applying mathematical planning systems to beverage and food manufacturing requires capturing specific processing limitations, such as two-stage preparation and packaging synchronization, line cleanings, and shelf-life constraints.1

### **1\. Two-Level Bottling Line Genetic Optimization**

* **Best Citation:** Cláudio Fabiano Motta Toledo, Paulo Morelato França, Reinaldo Morábito, and Alf Kimms, "Multi-population genetic algorithm to solve the synchronized and integrated two-level lot sizing and scheduling problem," *International Journal of Production Research* (IJPR), vol. 47, no. 11, pp. 3097-3119, 2009\.36 DOI: 10.1080/00207540701774431.  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~145-146.36  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Top-tier industrial engineering journal.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This is a validated case study of a real, named soft drink bottling plant in Brazil.36 The process involves two synchronized levels: liquid preparation in syrup tanks (Stage I) and high-speed packaging across parallel bottling lines (Stage II).40 This is structurally identical to our bubble tea factory, where boba syrup preparation (Stage I) must sync with Cup and Can packaging lines (Stage II).1  
* **Confidence:** High.36

### **2\. Integrated Beverage Scheduling and Bottle-Neck Alternation**

* **Best Citation:** Deisemara Ferreira, Reinaldo Morábito, and Socorro Rangel, "Solution approaches for the soft drink integrated production lot sizing and scheduling problem," *European Journal of Operational Research* (EJOR), vol. 196, no. 2, pp. 697-706, 2009\.40 DOI: 10.1016/j.ejor.2008.03.033.  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~228.42  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Top-tier operations research journal.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This highly cited paper proposes a mathematically exact MIP model for a real soft drink plant.40 It models sequence-dependent setup costs, line preparation times, and multi-stage synchronization where the production bottleneck alternates dynamically between liquid preparation and bottling lines.4 This validates our Layer 2 model, where parallel packaging lines are fed by the Semi-Finished Boba inventory.1  
* **Confidence:** High.40

### **3\. Synchronized Single-Stage Reformulations**

* **Best Citation:** Deisemara Ferreira, Alistair R. Clark, Bernardo Almada-Lobo, and Reinaldo Morábito, "Single-stage formulations for synchronised two-stage lot sizing and scheduling in soft drink production," *International Journal of Production Economics* (IJPE), vol. 136, no. 2, pp. 255-265, 2012\.41 DOI: 10.1016/j.ijpe.2011.11.020.  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~146.  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Top-tier operations research and economics journal.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This study improves upon two-stage scheduling by reformulating syrup preparation and packaging synchronization as a single-stage problem using the Asymmetric Traveling Salesman Problem (ATSP) framework.41 This provides a highly efficient modeling alternative that mirrors the computational acceleration needed in our Layer 2 scheduling model.1  
* **Confidence:** High.41

### **4\. Perishable Dairy Production and Distribution Routing**

* **Best Citation:** Aldair Alberto Alvarez Diaz, Jean-François Cordeau, Raf Jans, Pedro Augusto Munari Junior, and Reinaldo Morabito, "Formulations, branch-and-cut and a hybrid heuristic algorithm for an inventory routing problem with perishable products," *European Journal of Operational Research* (EJOR), vol. 283, no. 2, pp. 511-529, 2020\.43 DOI: 10.1016/j.ejor.2019.11.023.  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~26.43  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Top-tier operations research journal.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This paper addresses an integrated production, inventory, and logistics problem for a real Brazilian dairy plant, incorporating strict product perishability and shelf-life constraints.43 This provides a key reference for the multi-stage BOM dependencies and shelf-life limitations of milk-based boba ingredients.1  
* **Confidence:** High.43

| Reference | Manufacturing Environment | Critical Process Constraints | Modeling Methodology |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Toledo et al. (2009)** 36 | Soft drink bottling (Brazil) 36 | Tank setup times, raw material synchronization 36 | Multi-population genetic algorithm 36 |
| **Ferreira et al. (2009)** 40 | Soft drink plant (Brazil) 40 | Bottleneck alternation, sequence setups, liquid preparation 40 | Multi-stage mixed-integer programming (MIP) 40 |
| **Ferreira et al. (2012)** 41 | Beverage bottling 41 | Synchronized syrup-to-bottling, sequence cleaning 41 | ATSP-based single-stage formulations 41 |
| **Alvarez Diaz et al. (2020)** 43 | Dairy manufacturing 43 | Product perishability, parallel lines, shared storage 43 | Branch-and-Cut & Hybrid Heuristics 43 |

## **Q4: Methodological Integration of Two-Stage Stochastic Programming and Rolling Execution**

The core methodological novelty of the bubble tea system is the integration of a two-stage stochastic planning model (Layer 1\) with an operational rolling scheduling model (Layer 2).44 This exact integration can be validated using the following foundational references.

### **1\. The Exact Methodological Twin**

* **Best Citation:** Julian Englberger, Frank Herrmann, and Michael Manitz, "Two-stage stochastic master production scheduling under demand uncertainty in a rolling planning environment," *International Journal of Production Research* (IJPR), vol. 54, no. 20, pp. 6192-6215, 2016\.46 DOI: 10.1080/00207543.2016.1162917.46  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~6.46  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Top-tier industrial engineering journal.  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This is the *exact* methodological match for our paper's architecture. The authors propose a scenario-based, two-stage stochastic programming model with recourse for master production scheduling.45 They integrate this model directly into a rolling planning environment.45 Crucially, they modify standard stochastic programming formulations to prevent the rolling-induced infeasibilities that typically occur due to interdependencies with downstream scheduling layers.45 This supports our Layer 3 rolling execution and weekly replanning framework.7  
* **Confidence:** High.46

### **2\. Stochastic Production Routing with Rolling Execution**

* **Best Citation:** Yossiri Adulyasak, Jean-François Cordeau, and Raf Jans, "Benders decomposition for production routing under demand uncertainty," *Operations Research*, vol. 63, no. 4, pp. 851-867, 2015\.28 DOI: 10.1287/opre.2015.1387.  
* **Google Scholar Citation Count:** \~301.28  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Elite, top-tier operations research journal (UTD24/FT50 list).  
* **Relevance to our paper:** This highly cited paper integrates a two-stage stochastic programming model (handling tactical production setups and safety stocks) with a rolling horizon execution framework.28 It models baseline setups as first-stage decisions and continuous scheduling adjustments as second-stage recourse, validating the entire system through an out-of-sample simulation.28 This mirrors our Layer 1 and Layer 2 hierarchical integration.1  
* **Confidence:** High.28

## **Q5: Verification and Replacement of Weak Citations**

For a high-quality submission, citations in specialized or lower-tier journals must be replaced or supported by high-prestige, top-tier operations research alternatives.

### **1\. Evaluation and Replacement of Cruz et al. (2025)**

The reference to Jeferson Auto da Cruz, Luiz Leduino de Salles-Neto, and Cleder Marcos Schenekemberg (2025) exists and is verified 44:

* **Full Citation:** Jeferson Auto da Cruz, Luiz Leduino de Salles-Neto, and Cleder Marcos Schenekemberg, "A Rolling Horizon Approach for a Production Planning and Inventory Management Problem Under Stochastic Demand," *Process Integration and Optimization for Sustainability*, vol. 9, no. 5, pp. 2021-2046, June 2025\.44 DOI: 10.1007/s41660-025-00545-4.44  
* **Google Scholar Citations:** \~3.49  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Published by Springer Nature.44 It is a reputable, peer-reviewed engineering journal, but it is *not* a top-tier core OR/MS journal.  
* **Relevance:** It models a rolling horizon approach for dairy production under stochastic demand, incorporating shelf-life and inventory constraints.44  
* **Confidence:** High.47  
* **Top-Tier Replacement:** Lotte van Hezewijk, Nico P. Dellaert, and Willem L. van Jaarsveld, "Scalable deep reinforcement learning in the non-stationary capacitated lot sizing problem," *International Journal of Production Economics* (IJPE), vol. 284, Article 109434, 2025\.50 DOI: 10.1016/j.ijpe.2024.109434. This paper provides a highly prestigious, state-of-the-art reference for rolling horizon planning and lot-sizing under non-stationary stochastic demand.50

### **2\. Evaluation and Replacement of Mediouni et al. (2021)**

The reference to Abderrahmene Mediouni, Nicolas Zufferey, Mansour Rached, and Naoufel Cheikhrouhou (2021) exists and is verified 51:

* **Full Citation:** Abderrahmene Mediouni, Nicolas Zufferey, Mansour Rached, and Naoufel Cheikhrouhou, "The multi-period multi-level capacitated lot-sizing and scheduling problem in the dairy soft-drink industry," *Supply Chain Forum: An International Journal*, vol. 23, no. 3, pp. 272-284, 2022 (Published online Dec 2021).51 DOI: 10.1080/16258312.2021.2007735.51  
* **Google Scholar Citations:** \~13 53 (7 CrossRef citations 54).  
* **Journal Tier Assessment:** Reputable, peer-reviewed international supply chain journal, but it is *not* considered a top-tier core operations research journal.51  
* **Relevance:** This study focuses on an integrated multi-period multi-level lot-sizing and scheduling model for a real dairy soft-drink plant, synchronizing syrup tanks with parallel bottling lines under shelf-life limits.1  
* **Confidence:** High.51  
* **Top-Tier Replacement:** Alyne Toscano, Deisemara Ferreira, and Reinaldo Morabito, "A decomposition heuristic to solve the two-stage lot sizing and scheduling problem with temporal cleaning," *Flexible Services and Manufacturing Journal*, vol. 31, no. 1, pp. 142-173, 2019\.41 DOI: 10.1007/s10696-017-9293-1. This is an elite, highly-cited field journal in operations research. It models two-stage beverage lot-sizing and scheduling with parallel lines and cleanings, utilizing a decomposition heuristic that aligns directly with our hierarchical scheduling structure.41

## **Q6: Empirical Application and Computation of the Value of the Stochastic Solution (VSS)**

To demonstrate the mathematical value of the stochastic model, operations research papers require computing the Value of the Stochastic Solution (VSS).55 While Birge (1982) defines the theoretical metric, several studies have computed and reported VSS in real production planning and lot-sizing contexts.

### **1\. Mathematical Formulation of VSS in Production Planning**

To compute VSS for our 12-month horizon lot-sizing model, we define the following formulations 56:

1. **The Recourse Problem (RP):** Solves the full two-stage stochastic model, determining the first-stage setup decisions ![][image9] while taking into account expected second-stage volume adjustments ![][image10] across all scenarios ![][image11] 58:  
   ![][image12]  
   where ![][image13] represents the second-stage production, holding, and backlog costs.58  
2. **The Mean Value Problem (MVP):** Solves the deterministic lot-sizing problem where all stochastic demand scenarios are replaced by their expected values, ![][image14] 56:  
   ![][image15]  
   Let ![][image16] be the optimal first-stage solution to this deterministic model.56  
3. **The Expected Result of the Heuristic Expected Value Solution (EEV):** Fixes the first-stage setup decisions to ![][image16] and evaluates the expected cost of this plan across all stochastic demand scenarios ![][image11] 56:  
   ![][image17]

The Value of the Stochastic Solution (VSS) is then calculated as 56:  
![][image18]  
Because ![][image19] restricts first-stage setup options to a suboptimal deterministic plan, ![][image20] for minimization problems, guaranteeing that ![][image21].56

### **2\. Top-Tier Production Planning References Reporting VSS**

* **Yossiri Adulyasak, Jean-François Cordeau, and Raf Jans (2015):** In *Operations Research*, they explicitly calculate and report VSS for stochastic lot-sizing and scheduling, proving that the recourse model significantly outperforms deterministic EEV plans by avoiding out-of-sample backlogs and costly late adjustments.28  
* **Erkut Körpeoğlu, Hande Yaman, and M. Selim Aktürk (2011):** In *EJOR*, they compute VSS for master production scheduling.30 They prove that under tight capacity limits and high demand uncertainty, the VSS increases significantly, justifying the computational effort of stochastic programming.30  
* **Simon Thevenin, Yossiri Adulyasak, and Jean-François Cordeau (2021):** In *Production and Operations Management*, they evaluate VSS across various customer demand profiles.6 They demonstrate that joint optimization of lot sizes and safety stocks yields substantial out-of-sample cost savings (VSS) compared to decoupled MRP safety-stock heuristics.6

## **Synthesis and Strategic Recommendations for the Deployed System**

The literature analyzed provides strong theoretical and empirical support for the three-layer hierarchical stochastic production planning system deployed at the Turkish bubble tea manufacturer.

                                 
                                         │  
                                         ▼  
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│ LAYER 1: TWO-STAGE STOCHASTIC MPS (Monthly, 12-Month Horizon)                    │  
│  \- First-Stage (Months 1-2): Setup & Semi-Finished Boba production decisions      │  
│  \- Second-Stage (Months 3-12): Volume recourse under uniform sampling           │  
│  \- Objective: Min Expected (Holding \+ Backlog Cost)                              │  
└────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┘  
                                         │ Target Inventory Levels (Days 24 & 48\)  
                                         ▼  
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│ LAYER 2: DAILY MILP SCHEDULING (8-Week Rolling Horizon)                          │  
│  \- Decision variables: Binary machine-assignment (X\_m,j,t)                       │  
│  \- No separate lot-size variable: production fully determined by binary          │  
│  \- Exclusivity constraints (Cup vs. Can pasteurization capacity)                 │  
│  \- Linking constraints: Boba intermediate inventory targets                      │  
│  \- Two-Tier Backlog: Regular backlog and Critical backlog (\>12 days cancellation)│  
└────────────────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────┘  
                                         │ Replan Weekly (Every Monday)  
                                         ▼  
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  
│ LAYER 3: ROLLING HORIZON EXECUTION                                               │  
│  \- Stabilizes near-term decisions to mitigate schedule nervousness               │  
│  \- Resolves computational complexity (2,200s exact formulation reduced to live)  │  
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

By structuring the tactical Layer 1 model as a two-stage stochastic program and utilizing setup freezing (static setups, dynamic volumes), the system aligns with the state-of-the-art formulations developed by Thevenin et al. (2021).6 The daily MILP scheduling model in Layer 2 incorporates sequence-dependent setups and parallel line configurations that are highly characteristic of beverage processing operations.36 The use of linking inventory targets at Days 24 and 48 to coordinate Layer 1 and Layer 2 is supported by the multi-level scheduling frameworks of Toledo et al. (2009) and Ferreira et al. (2009).36 This prevents suboptimization and infeasibilities that typically plague decoupled hierarchical planning systems.45  
Finally, the rolling execution in Layer 3 provides a dynamic feedback loop that continuously incorporates updated demand forecasts, matching the receding-horizon control principles proven by Sethi and Sorger (1991).9 Weekly replanning stabilizes the schedule, mitigating system nervousness while maintaining computational feasibility.8 The reported ![][image1] total cost reduction and the ![][image4] dominance of the stochastic recourse model over deterministic approaches in the VSS analysis are consistent with the empirical benefits documented by Adulyasak et al. (2015) and Englberger et al. (2016).28 These findings confirm that integrating two-stage stochastic programming with rolling scheduling is a mathematically sound and highly effective strategy for complex manufacturing environments.28

#### **Alıntılanan çalışmalar**

11. Decision making scenarios in military transport processes, erişim tarihi Mayıs 25, 2026, [https://www.archivesoftransport.com/index.php/aot/article/view/413](https://www.archivesoftransport.com/index.php/aot/article/view/413)
